Then all the men ran round and round in the smoke, after which they gathered together and came on rushing towards the grave, yelling and throwing sticks and spears ahead of them. The idea of this was to drive the spirit, called mapuditti, into the grave. Then they stopped dancing and bent down, pointing at the grave in which the spirit was supposed to be watching them. The women formed into a procession and, with their deliberate movement and peculiar high knee action, danced round and round the audience of men who, as usual, were keeping time, striking their buttocks, stamping and singing. Everyone was very excited, but, between the dances, they laughed and talked as if they were taking part in an ordinary corrobboree, and it is evident that the whole cererhony is carried out with the object of pleasing the spirit of the dead person and also, at the same time, of intimating to it the fact that they expect it to remain quiet and not trouble them. After the water pouring is finished, the men take the charcoal or miornai, and rub themselves all over with it (Fig. 6i). No woman paints at this part of the ceremony, but all the men must, or else the spirit of the dead person would be angry and they would become ill. Only black must be used. The women bring in numbers of little cakes, called munduaii, made out of lily seeds. These were eaten by the older men only, the Umulakiri, or younger men, are not allowed to eat them..

evidence_summary: certainly enjoy, is over, the bones are carefully collected, wrapped in paper bark, and placed on a tree platform called kalla- kalla} If, for example, a Mumbali man dies, then Murungun men actually put them on the platform, while Kuial or Purdal men, his mother's brothers, stand below. As soon as anyone dies, the camps are immediately shifted, because the spirit, of whom they are frightened, haunts its old camping ground. The spirit part of a living man is called Jalkarra, and of a dead man padinia. For a long time after death the padinia roams about ' In the Nullakun tribe this is called Mungalla, and in the AUaua Allagalla. VI.

Then the men sway about, looking as fierce as they possibly can, while they place the wax spheres in the piendi, the kapei below the smaller ones. Away in the distance they can hear the spirit cursing and swearing, saying mulyarinyu koiyu ^ and using other opprobrious expressions. The men say nerk, nerk, nerk, and beckon it onwards. evidence_summary: the peindi, the front man knocks the stick representing it into the fire on the top of the korno. All of them shout. Ah, Ah, Ah; Ach, Ach, Bmg, Bmg ! at the top of their voices. Without a moment's pause, stones and earth are piled on the Yalmuru, one special large stone being placed on top, the men pressing down hard with all their might to keep it in. The spirit underneath can be heard sizzling and swearing (Fig. 6^). It tries to lift the stone but cannot. At length it is heard to say Grr, Grr, u-u. Then it is quiet and all is over.
